turns.text: Hello Andrea, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you wanting to listen. Honestly, it’s just… it’s a lot. Most days, I feel like I’m walking through a fog. I’m only twenty, and every time I look at those pamphlets the clinic gave me, the words just swim around on the page. I want this baby more than anything, but sometimes I sit on my bed and just stare at that bottle of folic acid on my nightstand, and I feel like I’m holding my future in my hands and I’m terrified I’m going to drop it.

It’s not just the fear of being a good mom; it’s the logistics. People talk about "prenatal care" like it’s just about showing up, but today, after that train delay, I sat on that hard plastic bench in the clinic and realized that for me, a simple checkup is an all-day mission. When the doctor mentioned my family’s history—the high blood pressure, the "medium risk" label—it felt like a warning that my body might not even be up to the task. 

Without Mami here… I don’t know. I feel like I’m missing the instruction manual everyone else seems to have. I’m doing this alone, and sometimes that silence in my apartment is louder than the city outside. I just want to know I’m going to be okay.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I guess I thought it would be more... personal, you know? Like in the movies, where there’s a doctor who holds your hand and tells you exactly what to do, like they’re part of the team. I expected that maybe someone would walk me through the "how-to" of being healthy, like how to manage everything without it feeling like a mountain.

In reality? It feels like being a number in a really busy, really crowded office. The nurse was nice, she really was, but she had a list to get through. She talked about my blood pressure and the folic acid like she was checking boxes on a form. And I get it—they're busy, and I'm just one person. But when she told me about needing fresh, healthy food, I almost laughed. How am I supposed to get to the grocery store across town when I’m barely holding down my shifts and the Blue Line is falling apart? 

It’s like they give me the *what*, but nobody talks about the *how*. They tell me to stay healthy, but they don’t see the three bus transfers it takes to find an apple that isn't bruised. I feel like they want me to be a "low-risk" patient, but my life makes that so hard to pull off. It’s a lot of instructions and not a lot of support.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest surprise was probably how fast everything turned into a medical problem. I walked into that clinic feeling hopeful—I mean, I was excited to finally take that first step toward starting a family—but within ten minutes, I wasn't "Andrea the future mom" anymore. I was just "Andrea, the medium-risk patient with a family history of hypertension." 

It caught me so off guard. I thought we’d talk about what to expect, or how to prepare my heart and my home, but instead, it was all about what could go wrong. It’s like they were already preparing for the worst-case scenario before I’d even started. Don't get me wrong, I know my health matters, and I know my family’s history is real. But being labeled "at risk" just felt so heavy. It made me feel like my own body was already acting like a stranger. 

I think the positive side of it was just realizing that someone *did* care enough to mention the folic acid, that at least there's a protocol to keep a baby safe. But mostly, it was just a punch to the gut. It made the whole thing feel less like a miracle and more like a high-stakes, stressful project that I’m not sure I have the energy to manage by myself.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I guess I expected to feel like a woman stepping into her power. I thought there would be this glow, this confidence—like, "I’m doing this, I’m building a life." I imagined myself as someone who would just know how to handle the nerves, someone who would be steady because the love for a child would just drown out any fear. I pictured myself being like Mami—solid, calm, and ready.

But the reality? It’s mostly just feeling small. I don’t feel like a woman stepping into power; I feel like a twenty-year-old girl who is completely out of her depth. The excitement is still there, deep down, but it’s suffocated by this cold, sharp anxiety. It’s not a glow; it’s a constant, low-level panic that I’m going to fail the baby before they’re even here.

I thought the hard part would be the midnight feedings or the crying—the stuff you see in the books. I didn't realize the hard part would be the isolation of *becoming* a mother. It’s like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff, and instead of feeling ready to jump, I’m just terrified that if I stumble, there’s nobody standing behind me to catch me. It’s lonely, and that’s not what I expected at all.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

What was missing? Honestly, someone to just see *me*. Not the "medium-risk" patient, not the file number, but the girl who’s grieving her mother while trying to figure out how to be one. 

When you’re twenty and you’re doing this alone, you aren't just looking for medical advice. You’re looking for someone to say, "I see you’re scared, and it’s okay to be scared." I needed a connection, not just a pamphlet. The professionals are so focused on the clinical side—the blood pressure, the pills, the tests—that they don't seem to notice that I'm literally shaking while I’m sitting there. A little bit of empathy would have gone a long way, like acknowledging that it’s hard to prioritize "fresh, healthy food" when your life feels like it’s being held together by duct tape and bus schedules.

And my friends… they’re sweet, but they’re in school, they’re going to parties, they’re living that carefree life. I can't talk to them about this. They don't know what it’s like to lose your Mami and then suddenly feel that massive, empty space where her advice should be. I needed someone to talk to, someone who could act as a bridge between the clinical world and the real, messy, lonely world I’m living in. I just feel like I’m fighting this battle on my own, with nobody in my corner to tell me I’m going to make it.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I thought I’d find something that felt like a conversation, or maybe a guide for someone like me—young, on a budget, and doing it solo. I imagined I’d find stories from other women who were also figuring things out on their own, maybe some practical tips on how to eat right when you don’t have a car or a whole pantry full of organic stuff.

What I actually found was… a lot of perfection. It’s all these beautiful, Pinterest-ready images of women in clean, sunlit rooms, drinking green smoothies, surrounded by partners who are rubbing their feet. It’s all about these expensive gadgets, tracking apps that demand you have a perfectly predictable life, and advice that seems meant for someone who doesn't have to worry about the Blue Line or the price of groceries. 

It didn't feel helpful; it felt like a mirror showing me everything I’m *not*. It made me feel poor and inadequate. When I look for information, I’m looking for someone to say, "I know you're tired, and here is how you manage your health when life is hard." Instead, I just find more reasons to feel like I’m failing because I can’t live up to the "ideal" pregnancy journey. It’s overwhelming, and honestly, it just makes the distance between where I am and where I want to be feel even wider.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I guess I expected the doctor to sit down with me and map it out. I thought they would explain, "Andrea, because of your family's history, we need to watch your blood pressure like this, and here is a plan to help you manage it even when things get stressful." I expected them to be my partners, to help me navigate the "how" of staying healthy. I thought they’d acknowledge the barriers—like how hard it is to get to the store or the toll of my commute—and help me find workarounds that actually fit my life.

In reality, it felt more like they just pointed at the problem and handed me a list of "thou shalt nots." They told me what my risks were—which, believe me, I already knew—but they didn't give me the tools to handle the stress that *causes* the blood pressure to spike in the first place. It was all theory. When I mentioned the struggle with getting to the grocery store, the nurse just gave me a sympathetic smile and said, "It's really important for the baby," as if I didn't already want that more than anything. 

It left me feeling like my health is just a checklist of vulnerabilities, and the responsibility for keeping those vulnerabilities from becoming disasters rests entirely on my shoulders. I felt judged for my circumstances instead of supported through them.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

That’s the part that hurts the most to talk about, honestly. I think every little girl, even if she doesn't say it out loud, imagines that when she finally starts her own family, there’s going to be someone there to hold her hand. I pictured having a partner who would be right there with me at the clinic—someone to hold the bags, someone to listen to the doctor so I didn't have to carry the weight of all those medical terms by myself. I imagined us looking at the pamphlets together, talking about names, and even just simple stuff, like him helping me figure out the grocery budget or the healthy cooking. I expected it to be a *we* thing.

But the reality is just... quiet. There is no partner. I’m doing this alone, and there’s no one to lean on when the train is late or when the doctor starts talking about risks. I’m the one who has to be both the mother and the person who supports the mother. It’s funny, I used to think that being "strong" meant doing it all by yourself, but now that I’m actually here, I realize that what I really wanted was someone to tell me they were proud of me, or just to ask how the appointment went. It’s a huge, empty space in my life that no clinic or pamphlet can fill.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

Every single moment. It feels like the entire weight of this future person is resting on my shoulders, and mine alone. It’s not just the big things, like the appointments or the blood pressure worries; it’s the constant, grinding maintenance of it all. 

When you’re single and you don’t have family nearby, there’s no one to tag-in. I’m the one navigating the bus routes to find a pharmacy that has what I need. I’m the one doing the math to see if I can afford the prenatal vitamins *and* my rent. I’m the one absorbing all the "what-ifs" the doctor throws at me. 

What drives that? It’s the lack of a safety net. I don't have a partner to help with the physical labor of the commute, and I don't have my Mami to guide me through the emotional labor of processing the fear. The healthcare system is built for a "standard" patient—someone who usually has a support person in the room. When you walk in alone, the doctors and nurses talk to *you* like you’re a grown woman who has everything sorted, forgetting that I’m just twenty and still learning how to be a person, let alone a mother. I’m the project manager, the patient, and the support system all at once, and some days, I don’t think I’m doing a very good job at any of it.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

When I decided to try again, I honestly expected that the doctors would remember—or at least look at my chart and see—that I’ve been through this heartbreak before. I expected there to be some acknowledgment of that loss, maybe a little extra care or even just a question asking how I was doing emotionally. I thought they would handle me with a bit more gentleness, knowing that every appointment is a minefield of "what if this happens again?" for me.

But the reality was cold. They were so focused on the *next* pregnancy and the clinical risks that my past loss felt completely invisible. Nobody asked how I was handling the anxiety of starting over, or if I needed someone to talk to about the fear that follows you around after you've lost a baby. It was like I was supposed to just wipe the slate clean and start fresh, like the grief didn't exist.

There wasn't a counselor, a support group, or even a nurse who sat with me for five extra minutes to talk about my feelings. It was all "let’s talk about your blood pressure" and "here’s your prescription." I felt like I was back in the same sterile, hurried office, just as alone as before. They treat your body like a machine that needs to be fixed, but they don't seem to realize that I’m carrying a broken heart along with the new hope.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I guess the only thing I’d want people to understand is that the medical stuff is only half the battle. You can give a woman all the folic acid in the world, you can give her all the pamphlets about healthy blood pressure, but if she’s crying on the Blue Line because she’s exhausted and has no one to talk to, she isn't really being "cared for."

I wish someone had just asked me, "Andrea, what do you need to make this possible?" not just "what do you need to do to be healthy?" There’s such a massive difference between those two questions. It’s about the life *around* the pregnancy—the transit, the grief for my Mami, the loneliness of my apartment. If you could fix the medical side of things but leave the human side broken, I’m not sure the medical side will even stick. 

I’m trying so hard to be the mom my baby deserves, but I’m just a girl trying to survive the day-to-day. I just hope that one day, the system realizes that a patient isn't just a body to be monitored—she’s a whole person with a whole life that she’s trying to carry. It would just mean so much if someone, somewhere, made me feel like I wasn't doing this entirely by myself.
